title: read_text_file silently truncates at 100_000 chars instead of paginating or erroring
description:
## Severity
Minor

## Dimension
Functional Correctness / Perimeter Defense

## Location
src/tools/file/read.rs:319-331 (read_text_file truncation branch)

## Violation
```rust
if result.len() > 100_000 {
    (
        format!("{}...\n(file truncated, {} total chars){}", safe_truncate(&result, 100_000), result.len(), suffix),
        false,    // <-- is_error = false
    )
}
```

The 100_000-char limit triggers silent (non-error) truncation. The test read_text_large_file_truncated_as_non_error (line 593) explicitly pins this as "OC truncation is NOT an error (CC parity gap)." Two problems:

1. The model cannot programmatically detect truncation — the text appended is informational only. It must read the message string to know the read was incomplete.
2. The truncation point is byte-based on `result`, which already contains the line-number prefixes (`"   N| "`). Truncating in the middle of a line-number prefix produces malformed output and breaks downstream edit_file lookups by line number.

## Mandated refactor
1. Truncate at a line boundary, not a byte boundary: `numbered.iter().scan(0, |bytes, line| { *bytes += line.len()+1; if *bytes > 100_000 { None } else { Some(line.clone()) } }).collect::<Vec<_>>()`.
2. Return is_error=true with a structured message instructing the model to retry with `offset`/`limit`. Or add a sentinel field in the typed ToolOutput (per the broader (String, bool) refactor) so the dispatcher can distinguish "incomplete success" from "complete success."

## Filed from QA review dispatch
